scenario: Iran re-closes Hormuz (Apr 18-19), strikes vessels; Islamabad talks rejected (IRNA Apr 19); ceasefire expires Apr 22 — no deal; Brent ~$96 est. (+4.5%; CNN ~$95.71 Apr 20); tail risk 42%; active vessel interdiction resumes full disruption narrative

categories.label: Food & Agriculture
categories.supplyChainImpact: Middle East urea and DAP fertilizer exports transit Hormuz, supplying ~30% of Asia's planted-area inputs. Shipping delays of 10-14 days via Cape route raise input costs and threaten planting windows for Vietnam, Thailand, and Indonesia rice belts. Palm oil - Asia's dominant cooking oil - faces surging logistics costs as Malaysia and Indonesia divert vessels.

assets.name: Wheat
assets.narrative: Wheat reverses on Iran Hormuz re-closure and IRGC vessel strikes. Blockade no longer vessel-scoped — active interdiction resumes full disruption mode. Gulf fertilizer flow normalization narrative collapses on talks rejection. Apr 22 ceasefire expiry is next catalyst. Source: Barchart ZWK26.

assets.name: Corn
assets.narrative: Biofuel correlation re-escalates with Brent ~$96 on Hormuz re-closure and talks rejection. Indonesian ethanol demand trajectory resumes upward pressure. Fertilizer and freight cost normalization narrative reversed — Apr 22 expiry sets next repricing window.

assets.name: Soybeans
assets.narrative: Bunker costs re-escalate with Brent ~$96 on Hormuz re-closure. Indonesian and Vietnamese crush margins compress again on freight and energy input reversal. Iran rejection of Apr 19 talks collapses deal-signal support for spread compression.

assets.name: Palm Oil (CPO)
assets.narrative: CPO firms as Brent crude correlation re-escalates to ~$96 on Hormuz re-closure. Biodiesel feedstock premium widens again. Indonesia B40 mandate adds structural floor. Fertilizer disruption for Q2 planting cycle resumes as Gulf urea normalization reverses. Source: Bursa Malaysia BMD.

assets.name: Rice (Thai 25% FOB)
assets.narrative: ASEAN food security pressure re-escalates as Hormuz re-closes and vessel strikes resume. Fertilizer normalization narrative collapses; Q2 planting season urea availability again at risk. Apr 22 ceasefire expiry without deal would extend supply chain disruption into planting season.

categories.label: Petrochemicals & Plastics
categories.supplyChainImpact: SABIC (Saudi), QAPCO (Qatar), and OCI (Oman) are among the world's largest petrochemical exporters - all ship via Hormuz. SE Asian crackers in Singapore, Thailand, and Malaysia rely on naphtha feedstock sourced from Gulf condensate. Downstream plastics, packaging, and agrochemicals face double squeeze: higher feedstock costs and logistics disruption.

assets.name: Naphtha (CFR Japan)
assets.narrative: Naphtha tracks Brent ~$96 higher on Hormuz re-closure and IRGC vessel strikes. Non-Iranian Gulf condensate flow now actively interdicted — blockade vessel-scoping reversed. Qatar force majeure persists; Ras Laffan restart not before late Aug 2026 (The National). Jurong Island and Map Ta Phut feedstock pressure re-escalates sharply.

assets.name: Ethylene (CFR NE Asia)
assets.narrative: Ethylene follows naphtha higher on Hormuz re-closure. SABIC and QAPCO export bottlenecks re-emerge as vessel interdiction resumes. Agricultural film and irrigation pipe lead times extending again. Source: ICE/ICIS CFR NE Asia (est.).

assets.name: Methanol (CFR China)
assets.narrative: Methanol firms on Hormuz re-closure. US Iran oil sanctions waiver expiry (Bloomberg Apr 14) tightens Iranian methanol enforcement. Qatar force majeure unresolved; Hormuz re-interdiction adds freight disruption layer. China MTO complexes face higher feedstock trajectory. Source: Investing.com CFR China (est.).

categories.label: Fertilizers
categories.supplyChainImpact: The Strait of Hormuz is the critical export artery for Middle East fertilizers: Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Oman, and UAE together account for ~18% of global urea supply and ~12% of DAP. QatarEnergy (Qafco) - world's largest urea exporter - declared force majeure on Mar 2. Iran is also a significant potash and urea producer. Asian planting windows create an acute near-term demand cliff.

assets.name: Urea (FOB Middle East)
assets.narrative: Urea spikes on Hormuz re-closure and talks rejection — full Gulf fertilizer export disruption resumes. Qafco force majeure persists; Ras Laffan restart not before late Aug 2026. US waiver expiry tightens Iranian-origin enforcement. Islamabad talks failure removes normalization timeline. Source: DTN, Argus Fertilizers (est.).

assets.name: DAP (FOB US Gulf)
assets.narrative: DAP reverses as Iran Hormuz re-closure raises Saudi export dislocation risk again. Saudi Ma'aden shipping remains disrupted; US Gulf supply stretched. Apr 22 ceasefire expiry is next pricing catalyst for wet-season planting in Philippines and Vietnam.

assets.name: Potash (MOP CFR SE Asia)
assets.narrative: Potash freight costs reverse higher with Hormuz re-closure as war-risk premiums re-escalate. Less directly exposed to Hormuz than urea or DAP — freight is the main transmission. Cape rerouting demand surges back; palm oil plantations in Malaysia and Indonesia face higher input cost trajectory again.

categories.label: Shipping & Freight
categories.supplyChainImpact: P&I club war risk coverage withdrawal (effective March 5) triggered mass tanker re-routing via Cape of Good Hope, adding 10-14 transit days and absorbing ~8% of global VLCC fleet capacity. Container lines have activated war risk surcharges and re-routed services, extending Asia-Europe transit times. Bunker fuel costs elevated by crude spike compound freight economics.

assets.name: Baltic Dry Index
assets.narrative: BDI reverses sharply on Iran Hormuz re-closure as Cape rerouting demand surges back. Normalization narrative collapses with talks rejected and ceasefire expiring Apr 22. P&I restoration timeline pushed out indefinitely pending political settlement. Source: Baltic Exchange via Hellenic Shipping News (est.).

assets.name: VLCC AG-Asia Rate
assets.narrative: VLCC rates spike on Hormuz re-closure and vessel strikes — insurance cost escalation resumes. Commercial Hormuz flows no longer partially preserved; active interdiction resumes. Apr 22 ceasefire expiry without deal would push rates to cycle highs. Source: Seatrade Maritime, Lloyd's List (est.).

assets.name: Container Freight (SCFI)
assets.narrative: Container rates spike on Hormuz re-closure. Asia-Europe normalization narrative reverses; Cape rerouting reinstated at full premium. Southeast Asian manufacturers face extended lead-time uncertainty through Apr 22 binary. Source: Shanghai Shipping Exchange (est.).
